prior_learning: Students will have covered some transformations, probability, area and volume in years 7 and 8 so it is important to ascertain what they remember and build on this.
common_misconceptions: Students struggle to visualise transformations.  With rotations  and enlargements they should be encouraged to use tracing paper to help.

When describing transformations students need to name the type of transformation and then the features.  If it is a translation they need to say that and then describe the vector.  If it is a rotation, they need to say that and then describe the amount of turn, the direction and the centre.
Students can get confused between theoretical and experimental probabilities – be specific about which you are dealing with.
Students need to understand that surface area is a 2D measurement and not a 3D one.  Units are square units. Use nets to teach SA to help them understand this.

key_knowledge_components: I know that the sum of all possible probabilities is 1.

key_vocab: chance, probability, probability scale, event, outcome

key_questions: See Peter’s statement under suggested teaching sequence.
Do you agree with Peter? Why/why not? Give me an example to help explain your reasons why/why not.
Give me an example of something that has a probability of 1?
Give me an example of an event that has a probability of 0? ½?
How many possible outcomes are there?
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: See questions under key questions to start the lesson. Talk about the probability scale eg Ask some simple probability questions around dice, spinners etc Then move to worded questions and increasingly complex questions eg Then move to probabilities of events not occurring to reinforce that the sum of all possible outcomes is 1 eg Finally move to ‘expected number’ style questions eg

notes.addressing_gaps_summary: SEND learners have a Pupil Passport that informs strategies for learning bespoke to that student. Staff must make sure they are familiar with the various strategies involved and embed these in their lessons. Probability questions often revolve around playing cards. If students do not play cards they won’t be familiar with how many suits there are, how many in a pack of cards etc. It is worth checking and spending time familiarizing students with…

learning_outcomes: To draw sample space diagrams and use them to find probabilities
key_knowledge_components: I can draw sample space diagrams and use them to find probabilities.

key_vocab: chance, probability, probability scale, event, outcome, sample space

key_questions: What possible outcomes are there?
How do we know we have listed all the possible outcomes?
What method could we use in this question so that we don’t forget any of the outcomes?
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Re-cap some simple probabilities. Introduce the idea of all possible outcomes and having a method to make sure you don’t leave any outcomes out eg a sample space diagram Give an example of a 2 way table as a form of sample space diagram eg for when you are throwing 2 coins, what’s the probability of getting 2 heads? Answer = ¼.  The 2 way table shows that there are 4 possible outcomes with 1 chance in 4 of you throwing 2 heads. H T H H, H

notes.addressing_gaps_summary: SEND learners have a Pupil Passport that informs strategies for learning bespoke to that student. Alternatively, give them questions with the headings populated and remove this sup…

key_knowledge_components: I understand sets and set notation.

key_questions: Where in the diagram would I put the number 2? Why?
Why does 4 go in the middle? See diagram below.
What do I mean by the intersection?
What do I mean by union? Complement?
Can you give me an example of a number that would not go in this Venn diagram?
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Start by getting students to choose where to place numbers in the Venn diagram Sets can be shown visually through the use of a Venn diagram or numerically.  A Venn diagram makes it easier to categorise within two or more sets eg Give students examples to practise: Explain that in sets you can also write the numbers in the set as a list eg Then ask students probability questions using Venn diagrams to help them answer. learning_outcomes: Draw and interpret Venn diagrams, and use them to calculate probabilities.
key_knowledge_components: I can draw and interpret Venn diagrams, and use them to calculate probabilities.

key_questions: What’s the probability a person from this class has brown hair?
What’s the probability they have brown hair and glasses?
In this question which are the important pieces of information?
Which pieces of information are redundant?
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Start with a re-cap of previous lesson by asking probability questions from simple Venn diagrams Next move to sets within sets eg In this example we are only choosing from the set of students with brown hair eg 4 students.  The probability is therefore ¼ that they will also have glasses. Set up some Venn diagrams to populate as a class eg Before getting students some to set up and draw themselves.

key_knowledge_components: I can carry out reflections, rotations, translations and enlargements.
I can describe a given transformation using mathematical language.

key_vocab: .Reflection, transformation, line of symmetry, object(A), image (A’)

key_questions: Which shape is the object and which is the image? How do you know?
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Start by modelling some simple reflections, with vertical lines of symmetry Then show some with horizontal lines of symmetry and show how it’s easier to visualise if you turn the page around so that the line of symmetry is vertical. Likewise for any diagonal lines of symmetry.  Give students some practice at drawing the image for a given object and line of symmetry. Start to teach equations of lines of symmetry by getting students to look for pa…

Staff must make sure they are familiar with the various strategies involved and embed these in their lessons. Some students may need access to a mirror so that they can visualise where the reflection will fall. Remind students that when they are undertaking any reflection if they turn the page so that the line of symmetry is vertical it is easier to…

key_knowledge_components: I can carry out reflections, rotations, translations and enlargements.
I can describe a given transformation using mathematical language.

key_vocab: Reflection, line of symmetry, object, image, rotation, centre of rotation, clockwise, anticlockwise, coordinate

key_questions: Which way am I turning – clockwise or anticlockwise?
If I turn 90o clockwise which way will I be facing?
What about if I turn 180o?
What angle is the same as 180o clockwise? (The same angle anti-clockwise)
What 3 pieces of information do I need in order to describe a rotation?
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Start by modelling some simple rotations, checking on understanding of clockwise/anticlockwise and that students can estimate rotations of 45, 90, 180, 270 degrees. Then give students some simple rotations to practice with, using tracing paper to help with accuracy. If they trace the shape and put their pencil on the centre of rotation before rotating it is easy to see where the image will be. Then introduce examples where the centre of rotation…

key_questions: What types of transformations are there?
What do we call the transformation when we move a shape?
How would we write the vector?
How far is it from this point to this vertex? How many spaces right and then how many spaces up do we need to go?
What 3 pieces of information to you need to give when describing an enlargement? (state it is an enlargement, scale factor, centre of enlargement)
Does an enlargement always make a shape bigger? (ans = No, fractional enlargements make a shape smaller)
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Students will have experience of translations so start by re-capping vectors and translations. Check that they understand vectors involving negatives. Then give students practice at translating shapes by different vectors. Then move to describing translations. learning_outcomes: Recognise and describe 3D shapes using mathematical properties.
key_knowledge_components: I can find the volume and surface area of 3D shapes, Including cylinders.

key_vocab: Face, edge, vertex, vertices, cube, cuboid, prism, cylinder, clone, sphere, square based pyramid, triangular based pyramid, net, volume, cubic units, length, width, depth, height,

key_questions: What’s the difference between a cube and a cuboid?
Tell me a real life shape that is a cylinder? Eg toilet roll
What real life shape is a sphere? Eg, football
When we find the volume what exactly are we measuring?
What units would we use? Why?
Is there a quicker way of calculating volume rather than counting the cubes?
What 3 dimensions do we multiply together? Why?
How does the area link to the volume?
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: A s a starter activity re-cap the names of common 3D shapes Ask students questions about the shapes using their properties eg., You could play a game of Guess the Shape by listing properties one by one to see who can guess the shape first eg., Move onto calculating the volume of a cuboid. Start by counting cubes, checking that students understand it is a 3D measurement and the units will be cubic regardless of whether they are cm, km m etc. Then…

key_knowledge_components: I can find the volume and surface are of 3D shapes, Including cylinders.

key_vocab: Face, edge, vertex, vertices, cube, cuboid, prism, cylinder, clone, sphere, square based pyramid, triangular based pyramid, net, volume, cubic units, length, width, depth, height,

key_questions: What does the cross section of a cylinder look like?
What about a cuboid?
Is a cuboid a prism? Why?
Is a sphere a prism? Why?
What does the cross-section of this 3D prism look like?
What everyday objects are cylinders?
How can I use the area to help me find the volume?
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Start by clarifying what the definition of a prism is If you add depth to a 2D shape you get a prism To calculate the volume of a prism find the area of the cross section and multiply by the depth Model with a few shapes and then check students understand. Eg find the volume of this 3D shape Give students examples to practise themselves.

notes.addressing_gaps_summary: SEND learners have a Pupil Passport that informs strategies for learning bespoke to that student. Staff must make sure they are familiar with the various strategies involved and embed these in their lessons. Use multilink cubes to help students visualise the prisms.  You can use these to show the different layers of the prism so that they understand the concept of depth.
notes.deepening_knowledge_summary: Give students questions with more complex volumes eg trapezoidal prisms Week/ Lesson Key Question? learning_outcomes: Surface area of a cuboid and a prism
key_knowledge_components: I can describe the difference between volume and surface area.
I know what Pythagoras’ Theorem is used for.
I can use Pythagoras’ Theorem to find missing sides of right angled triangles.

key_vocab: Face, edge, vertex, vertices, cube, cuboid, prism, cylinder, clone, sphere, square based pyramid, triangular based pyramid, net, volume, cubic units, length, width, depth, height, surface area
theorem, hypotenuse, square, square root, formula, right angle, surd

key_questions: What’s the difference between the volume and the surface area of a 3D shape?
What units do we use with surface area?
What does the net of a cuboid look like?
What does the net of a cube look like? What’s the area of one face of this cube? How many faces are there? So, how do I calculate the surface area?
A cube has a surface area of 54 cm2.  What is its volume?
Which side is the hypotenuse?
Sometimes it is better to leave your answer in surd form – why do you think this is?
(When you square root a number that doesn’t have an exact answer we round it but every time we round we create a small amount of error).
Calculate the missing side. Why is this question different? How would we solve it?
Is this triangle right angled? Prove your answer.
teaching.suggested_activities_summary: Start by looking at the difference between a volume and a surface area. Get students to sketch the net of a cube and then calculate the area of one face.  So to find the surface area of a cube it is 6 times the surface area of a face as each face is the same. Move onto a cuboid and encourage students to sketch nets before calculating the surface area.  On a cuboid each face will have another face with the same area. Next look at triangular prism…

Staff must make sure they are familiar with the various strategies involved and embed these in their lessons. Use physical 3D shapes to help students visualise and sketch the nets. notes.deepening_knowledge_summary: Use more complex shapes to find the surface areas.  Use the same method where they sketvch the net and find the area of each face before finding the total.
